criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the CLIP architecture including image and text encoders, cosine similarity scoring, and default prompt structure.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the Parameter-Free Prompt Tuning (PFPT) framework and its coarse-to-fine strategy with Single Token Search and Parallel Tokens Search.
The agent has read Section 3.2.1 to understand how adversarial examples are generated on-the-fly during training using gradient-based PGD perturbations.

The agent has read Section 3.2.2 to understand the parameterization of defense tokens as K tokens appended to the class prompt from the pre-trained vocabulary, and the overall search objective (Equation 6).
The agent has read Section 3.2.3 and Algorithm 2 to understand the Single Token Search (STS) procedure, including the gradient-based defense objective in Equation 7 and the top-L candidate selection.

The agent has read Section 3.2.4 and Algorithm 3 to understand the Parallel Tokens Search (PTS) procedure, including candidate concatenation (Equation 8), the consistency score (Equations 9-10), the accuracy score (Equation 11), and the total scoring formula.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 to check the adversarial attack procedure: uniform perturbation initialization in [-ε, ε], iterative PGD updates with step size η, sign gradient update, and L-inf projection.

The agent has read Section 4 to understand the full experimental setup: 11 in-distribution datasets, ViT-B/32 backbone with TeCoA pre-trained weights, two perturbation budgets (ε = 1/255 and ε = 4/255), and 1-/4-/16-/100-shot sampling strategies.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the average in-distribution accuracy and robustness results for HEP, APT, and PFPT across various ε and shot settings.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the generalization evaluation results on distribution shift datasets (ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) and the cross-dataset average on 10 other datasets.
The agent has read Figure 5 and Figure 6 to check the per-dataset in-distribution performance and the accuracy-robustness trade-off results.
The plan includes loading a pre-trained CLIP model (ViT-B/32) with TeCoA adversarially pre-trained image encoder weights, keeping all model parameters frozen during defense token search.
The plan includes implementing feature extraction using CLIP's image and text encoders as in Equation 1, producing d-dimensional feature vectors.
The plan includes implementing cosine similarity-based classification probability with temperature parameter τ as in Equation 2.
The plan includes parameterizing K defense tokens appended as suffixes (or prefixes) to the default text prompt, with tokens selected from the pre-trained CLIP vocabulary as in Equation 5.
The plan includes implementing the adversarial attack procedure (Algorithm 1) for on-the-fly adversarial example generation, with 3 PGD steps and step size 2ε/3 during training.
The plan includes implementing the Single Token Search (STS) to compute the gradient-based defense objective for each token position and select top-L candidate tokens per position.

The plan includes implementing the Parallel Tokens Search (PTS) to concatenate same-rank tokens across positions, score each candidate combination by consistency and accuracy, and select the lowest-score combination.
The plan includes implementing the consistency score using KL divergence between predictions with and without defense tokens on clean images as in Equations 9-10.
The plan includes implementing the accuracy score as an indicator function for correct classification on adversarial examples as in Equation 11.
The plan includes implementing the total score formula L^total = L^consist + λ(1 - L^acc) for PTS candidate ranking, selecting the minimum-score candidate as the final defense tokens.

The plan includes preparing all 11 in-distribution datasets (ImageNet, Caltech101, OxfordPets, StanfordCars, Flowers102, Food101, FGVCAircraft, SUN397, DTD, EuroSAT, UCF101) with N-shot random sampling from training sets.
The plan includes setting up evaluation using PGD with 100 steps and step size ε/4 for measuring adversarial robustness.

The plan includes implementing baseline methods: HEP (CLIP's default 'a photo of a [CLASS]' prompt) and APT (continuous text embedding optimization converted to nearest vocabulary word by Euclidean distance).

The plan includes setting up generalization evaluation by training defense tokens on ImageNet with 16-shots and evaluating on ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R (distribution shift) and 10 other datasets (cross-dataset).
The code for loading the CLIP ViT-B/32 model with TeCoA adversarially pre-trained image encoder weights has been implemented, with all model parameters frozen during the search process.
The code for computing image features f^v and text features f^t via CLIP's visual encoder E_v and text encoder E_t has been implemented as in Equation 1.
The code for computing cosine similarity-based alignment probability between image and text features using temperature τ has been implemented as in Equation 2.
The code for constructing class text prompts T'_j with K defense tokens appended after the class token has been implemented as in Equation 5.

The code for generating adversarial examples following Algorithm 1 has been implemented, including: uniform initialization of δ in [-ε, ε], S iterative PGD steps, signed gradient update with step size η, and L-inf projection back to the ε-ball.
The code for the adversarial attack objective (Equation 4) maximizing the loss between the adversarial image feature and the ground-truth text feature has been implemented.
The code for the overall search objective (Equation 6) minimizing the expected loss over adversarial examples in a batch has been implemented.

The code for the Single Token Search (STS) defense objective (Equation 7) computing the gradient of the log-likelihood loss with respect to the one-hot embedding of each candidate token t_k has been implemented.

The code for constructing K candidate sets {C_{k,l}} by selecting the top-L tokens ranked by descending defense objective scores for each of the K defense token positions has been implemented following Algorithm 2.
The code for concatenating same-rank tokens across all K positions to form parallel candidate defense token sets A_l has been implemented as in Equation 8.

The code for computing the consistency score between predictions with defense tokens (q^1) and without defense tokens (q^2) on clean images has been implemented as in Equations 9-10, using KL divergence.

The code for computing the accuracy score as an indicator function checking correct classification of adversarial examples when using prompts with defense tokens has been implemented as in Equation 11.

The code for computing the total score L^total(t') = L^consist(t') + λ(1 - L^acc(t')) and selecting the minimum-score candidate from {A_l} as the final defense tokens has been implemented following Algorithm 3.

The code for loading, preprocessing, and N-shot sampling of all 11 in-distribution datasets (ImageNet, Caltech101, OxfordPets, StanfordCars, Flowers102, Food101, FGVCAircraft, SUN397, DTD, EuroSAT, UCF101) has been implemented.
The code for evaluating adversarial robustness using PGD with 100 attack steps and step size ε/4 at test time has been implemented.
The code for evaluating standard (clean) accuracy and adversarial robustness as the two reported metrics has been implemented.
The code for the HEP baseline using CLIP's default prompt 'a photo of a [CLASS]' without any learned defense tokens has been implemented.

The code for the APT baseline optimizing continuous text embeddings for adversarial robustness and converting to nearest vocabulary word by Euclidean distance for fair comparison has been implemented.
The code for running PFPT with K=16 defense tokens and both position settings (prefix and suffix) has been implemented.
The code for using 3 PGD steps with step size 2ε/3 for on-the-fly adversarial example generation during the defense token search (training phase) has been implemented.
The code for loading the three distribution shift evaluation datasets (ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) for the generalization evaluation has been implemented.
The code for comparing adversarially-trained defense tokens against standardly-trained prompts to measure the accuracy-robustness trade-off has been implemented.
The in-distribution PFPT defense token search has been executed successfully for all 11 datasets across 1-, 4-, 16-, and 100-shot settings with both ε = 1/255 and ε = 4/255.
The HEP and APT baseline evaluations have been executed successfully on all 11 datasets across 1-, 4-, 16-, and 100-shot settings with both ε = 1/255 and ε = 4/255.
The generalization evaluation of defense tokens learned on ImageNet (16-shot, ε = 4/255) has been executed successfully on distribution shift datasets (ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R).
The cross-dataset generalization evaluation of defense tokens learned on ImageNet has been executed successfully on the remaining 10 datasets.
The accuracy-robustness trade-off evaluation comparing adversarially-trained vs. standardly-trained prompts has been executed successfully on all 11 datasets under 16-shot and ε = 4/255.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: PFPT outperforms HEP with +4.49% accuracy and +5.58% robustness under 1-shot setting at ε = 1/255 (PFPT: 49.41% Acc., 37.51% Rob. vs. HEP: 44.92% Acc., 31.93% Rob.).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: PFPT consistently outperforms APT across all shot settings (1, 4, 16, 100-shot) and both perturbation budgets (ε = 1/255 and ε = 4/255).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: APT underperforms HEP in the 4-shot setting under ε = 1/255, showing APT requires more data to converge.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: PFPT achieves 50.36% accuracy and 38.32% robustness in the 100-shot setting at ε = 1/255, showing consistent gains with more training data.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: PFPT achieves the highest accuracy and robustness on the source ImageNet dataset (44.9% Acc., 14.8% Rob.) under 16-shots and ε = 4/255.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: PFPT outperforms HEP and APT on all three distribution shift datasets (ImageNet-V2: 34.5%/9.6%, ImageNet-Sketch: 18.9%/8.0%, ImageNet-R: 22.3%/6.5%).

The execution outcomes match Table 2: PFPT achieves the best cross-dataset average performance (33.6% Acc., 12.0% Rob.) among all methods, while APT (31.1% Acc.) underperforms HEP (32.3% Acc.) on cross-dataset evaluation.

The execution outcomes confirm Figure 6: PFPT adversarially-trained prompts improve robustness while incurring a minor accuracy decrease compared to standardly-trained prompts on most of the 11 datasets, with OxfordPets showing the largest robustness gain (+10.3%).
The execution outcomes confirm Figure 5: PFPT outperforms baseline methods across all 11 datasets at ε = 4/255, K = 16 in the averaged performance chart.
